title: Java News Roundup: Hardwood 1.0, Endive 1.0, Azul Payara, Quarkus, WildFly, LangChain4j, OSSI
author: Michael Redlich
gist: A dense weekly digest of the Java/JVM ecosystem covering JDK 27/28 early-access builds, security fixes in Azul Payara, new Quarkus and WildFly features, LangChain4j adding an agentic 'Debate' pattern, Asymm Systems' new Eliya JDK distribution, GA releases of the Hardwood Parquet parser and Endive Wasm runtime, and the launch of an open-source sustainability initiative backed by HeroDevs and Commonhaus Foundation.

insights.claim: JDK 27 Build 28 and JDK 28 Build 4 early-access builds were both released in the same week (around June 22, 2026), each containing bug fixes over their prior builds, indicating parallel active development streams for two future JDK LTS/feature releases.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: Confirms OpenJDK's multi-release-ahead development cadence is on track; useful for enterprises planning JVM upgrade timelines against JDK 27/28 milestones.
insights.quote: Build 28 of the JDK 27 early-access builds was made available this past week featuring updates from Build 27 that include fixes for various issues.

insights.claim: Azul Payara's June 2026 release (7.1.0 across Community, Enterprise 6.39.0, and Enterprise 5.88.0 editions) patched a CSRF/SSRF vulnerability in the Admin Console and REST management interface caused by unnecessarily passing the REST URL in a servlet request, which was otherwise obtainable via session information.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: A concrete CSRF/SSRF CVE fix across all three Payara editions simultaneously — enterprises running Payara admin consoles should prioritize patching given the security-sensitive nature of the admin/REST management surface.
insights.quote: a resolution to a Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) issue in the Admin Console and REST management interface due to unnecessary passing the REST URL in a servlet request that can otherwise be obtained via session information

insights.claim: LangChain4j 1.17.0 added a DebatePlanner class specifically to support a 'Debate' agentic pattern (multiple agents arguing/reasoning against each other), alongside a new OracleChatMemoryStore for persisting chat memory in an Oracle database and a raw streaming event callback for unmapped events.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: Signals that multi-agent debate/deliberation patterns are maturing into first-class library primitives in the Java AI ecosystem, and that Oracle is positioning its database as agent memory infrastructure — watch for Oracle's broader AI-agent infra push.
insights.quote: a new DebatePlanner class to support the Debate agentic pattern; a new OracleChatMemoryStore class that adds an Oracle database for chat memory

insights.claim: Quarkus 3.37.0 introduced an experimental JLink extension that generates a custom Java runtime image containing only the necessary JDK modules for an application, alongside reflection-free Jackson serializers enabled by default.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: Smaller custom runtime images (via jlink) and reflection-free serialization both reduce container image size and startup latency — relevant to cloud-cost and cold-start optimization for Java microservices/serverless deployments.
insights.quote: a new experimental extension, JLink, that generates a custom runtime image with jlink that includes only the necessary JDK modules in an application; and enabling reflection-free Jackson serializers by default

themes.theme: New JVM-native runtimes: Wasm and Parquet

insights.claim: Endive 1.0.0, a JVM-native WebAssembly runtime descended from the Chicory project (created September 2023 by Dylibso CTO Benjamin Eckel and IBM's Andrea Peruffo), shipped with WasmGC host integration (externref as a Java Object) and an optimization eliminating unnecessary stack frame allocation for tail calls, benefiting interpreter loops like CPython's.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: A maturing JVM-native Wasm runtime with GC and tail-call support widens the JVM's ability to host non-Java/Wasm-compiled languages efficiently — relevant to polyglot runtime strategy for JVM shops and to Dylibso/IBM's Wasm tooling investments.
insights.quote: WasmGC host integration with support for the externref value type as a Java Object such that host functions work naturally with GC types

insights.claim: Hardwood 1.0.0, a dedicated Apache Parquet file format parser, fixed a performance regression by making close() idempotent on ColumnReader and RowReader, corrected column-projection handling for DECIMAL/UUID/UINT_32/FIXED types, and now accepts a HardwoodContext implementation for controlling decoder thread-pool sizing and sharing context across readers.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: A new dedicated, performance-tunable Parquet parser for the JVM ecosystem is relevant to any data-engineering stack (Spark, Flink, Trino alternatives) evaluating faster or more controllable Parquet I/O libraries.
insights.quote: an instance of the AvroRowReader class now honors column projections such that the DECIMAL, UUID, UINT_32, and FIXED columns are correctly read

insights.claim: HeroDevs joined the Commonhaus Foundation as founding member of a new Open Source Sustainability Initiative (OSSI) aimed at improving lifecycle transparency and collaboration between maintainers, foundations, and ecosystem partners; HeroDevs already partners with Hibernate, Jackson, and Quarkus communities on CVE remediation for end-of-life open-source software.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: HeroDevs' business model (paid EOL-software CVE remediation) is institutionalizing itself via foundation partnerships — a commercial open-source-sustainability model investors should track as a template beyond the Java ecosystem.
insights.quote: HeroDevs, the platform that identifies, remediates and prevents end-of-life open source risk, announced that they have joined the Commonhaus Foundation as the founding member of the Open Source Sustainability Initiative (OSSI)

insights.claim: Asymm Systems introduced Eliya JDK, a downstream OpenJDK distribution tracking quarterly OpenJDK 25 Critical Patch Updates, with initial release 25.0.3 based on the Oracle Critical Patch Update Advisory for April 2026, delivering builds for Linux x86_64 and Linux aarch64.
insights.speaker: Michael Redlich
insights.so_what: Another new entrant in the crowded OpenJDK distribution market (alongside Adoptium, Azul, Amazon Corretto, Red Hat) — worth tracking whether Eliya's compliance/diagnostics angle carves out real enterprise share versus commoditized JDK builds.
insights.quote: Asymm Systems has introduced Eliya JDK, their downstream distribution of OpenJDK that tracks the quarterly OpenJDK 25 Critical Patch Updates (CPUs) and delivers builds for Linux x86_64 and Linux aarch64.
